questions.prompt: A customer with 60 staff already in their Microsoft 365 tenant is migrating to PSE. They want the fastest path that keeps user records in sync with M365. Which path fits?
choices: Manual single-extension creation, one user at a time
Bulk CSV import after exporting users from M365
Microsoft 365 user sync with the Map section configured to keep all five default fields synced
Bulk CSV import, then enabling M365 SSO after the fact
questions.explanation: M365 user sync uses Entra ID as the source of truth and keeps the fields aligned ongoing. Bulk CSV produces PBX-local records that drift from M365 over time. Manual is too slow at this scale. M365 SSO without sync is also possible but doesn't propagate identity changes back to PBX records.

questions.prompt: The customer's ITSP provides a SIP service that authenticates by source IP address (no username or password). Which PSE trunk type fits?
choices: Register Trunk
Peer Trunk
Account Trunk
WebRTC Trunk
questions.explanation: Peer Trunk uses IP-based authentication; the ITSP whitelists the PBX's IP. Register Trunk needs username and password (PBX registers to the ITSP). Account Trunk is for a third-party device registering inward to the PBX.

questions.prompt: A user reports that pressing keys in the carrier's voicemail IVR does nothing. The call audio is fine. What's the most likely cause?
choices: The codec preference list doesn't include G.711
DTMF Mode mismatch between the trunk and the carrier (e.g. RFC 4733 vs Inband)
P-Early-Media is disabled
Maximum Concurrent Calls on the trunk is too low
questions.explanation: DTMF Mode controls how key presses travel. Most carriers default to RFC 4733 (RFC 2833), which carries DTMF in RTP packets. If the trunk is set to Inband and the carrier expects RFC 4733, the presses are inside the audio stream and the IVR can't pick them out cleanly.

questions.prompt: A customer has 50 sequential DIDs (8821001-8821050) that should each ring the matching extension (1001-1050). Which approaches will work? (Select all that apply.)
choices: DID Matching Mode: DID Pattern, with 50 individual patterns
DID Matching Mode: Match DID Range to Extension Range, single rule
DID Matching Mode: Match DID Pattern to Extensions, pattern 882{{.Ext}}
DID Matching Mode: DID Number to Specific Extension, 50 explicit mappings
Create 50 separate inbound routes, one per DID
questions.explanation: Match DID Range to Extension Range is the cleanest (one rule covers all 50). Match DID Pattern to Extensions with the {{.Ext}} placeholder also works for sequential DIDs. DID Number to Specific Extension works but is verbose. Fifty separate routes works but is even more verbose. The first option (DID Pattern with 50 patterns) is theoretically possible but unmaintainable.

questions.prompt: The pattern +61[2378]XXXXXXXX is used on an inbound route. Which dialled number does it match?
choices: +611988887777 (only 11 digits after the +)
+61298887777 (matches the 2 area code)
+6129888777 (one digit short)
questions.explanation: The pattern requires a leading +61 then one of [2,3,7,8] then exactly 8 more digits (XXXXXXXX). +61298887777 fits: +61, then 2, then 98887777. The 4 area code isn't in the bracket set. The other options have the wrong digit count.

questions.prompt: The PBX's system timezone and the per-route Time Zone field can be set to different values, which is why misrouted calls during business hours often trace back to a timezone disagreement between these two settings.
questions.explanation: They are independent. The PBX-wide setting and the per-route setting can disagree, producing surprising routing. Always check both when investigating a 'wrong destination at this time of day' ticket.

questions.prompt: Sort the four PSE IVR 'Dial Extensions' modes from most restrictive (least caller reach) to least restrictive (most caller reach).
questions.explanation: Disable is the absolute floor (no extension reachable). Allowed Extensions is a tight allowlist. Restricted Extensions is a denylist (everything reachable minus the named exceptions), which is more permissive because anything not on the list is open by default. All Extensions exposes every extension including service extensions (intercom, printer) which is the common abuse vector. The right default for most customers is Allowed Extensions tied to the published staff directory.
items: Disable (callers cannot dial any extension from the IVR)
Allowed Extensions (only the curated list is reachable)
Restricted Extensions (everything is reachable except the curated deny list)
All Extensions (every extension on the PBX is reachable)

questions.prompt: A customer-facing support queue has eight agents of similar skill. The customer wants the workload distributed fairly so that nobody gets every other call. Which ring strategy fits?
choices: Linear
Ring All
Least Recent
Random
questions.explanation: Least Recent picks the agent who has been idle longest, which spreads calls evenly. Linear always starts with the first agent in the list (top of list takes all the calls until they're busy). Ring All rings everyone at once. Random is genuinely fair on average but produces uneven load in short windows.

questions.prompt: Select the scenarios that need a queue rather than a ring group. (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: Queues are the right tool when wait time, answered rate, callers-in-queue announcements, or agent skill tagging are part of the operational model. Ring groups handle the simpler 'a fixed set of people, anyone can pick up' or 'sequential ringing with a delay' patterns without queue overhead. Reception backline = ring group. On-call rota with sequential delay = ring group with the right strategy. SLA-bound customer support and skill-routed multi-language teams need queue features.
choices: Three-person reception backline in one office, anyone can pick up, no metrics needed
After-hours on-call rota with two engineers, second engineer rings 15s after first
Customer-facing support service with SLA on answer rate; MoH and position announcements expected
Multi-language customer service with skill-tagged agents and skill-based routing

questions.prompt: The customer wants recordings of Sales queue calls available to the Sales Manager, but NOT to other staff or other managers. Which permission scope on the Sales Manager role fits?
choices: Recording Files: Play; scope = All Extensions
Recording Files: Play; scope = All other extensions of the same department
Recording Files: Play + Download + Delete; scope = All Extensions
Recording Files: Play; scope = Specific Extensions (none selected)
questions.explanation: 'All other extensions of the same department' scopes the playback to the manager's own department's recordings. All Extensions exposes recordings the Sales Manager shouldn't see. The Specific Extensions with none selected blocks everything. Delete and Download permissions weren't asked for and shouldn't be added.

questions.prompt: A customer with seven-year recording retention should configure that retention by setting Recordings Preservation Days to 2555 on the PBX's local storage.
questions.explanation: Local storage on the PBX won't hold seven years of recordings for any non-trivial customer. The right pattern is short local retention (30-90 days) plus an archive task pushing recordings to S3, SharePoint, or SFTP, with archive-side retention covering the seven-year requirement. Delete-local-after-archiving keeps local storage manageable.

questions.prompt: An outbound call connects, the customer can hear the called party, but the called party can't hear the customer. SIP trace shows symmetric SDP from both ends with public IPs in the c= lines, codec G.711 agreed. RTP packets leave PBX toward the public IP of the called party. What's the most likely cause to investigate next?
choices: Recompile the trunk codec list to remove G.729
SIP ALG (or 'SIP helper') on an in-path NAT router rewriting SDP or mangling RTP port translations
Switch the trunk's DTMF Mode to Auto
The customer's office firewall rate-limiting outbound UDP packets in the RTP port range
questions.explanation: One-way audio with clean SDP is the canonical NAT-traversal symptom and SIP ALG is the most common cause. An ALG can rewrite the c= line so it looks correct in the trace, yet still close the RTP pinhole prematurely or reassign source ports so the far end drops packets on symmetric-RTP checks. First move: identify NAT routers between the PBX and the trunk and disable SIP ALG (it should always be off for a SIP trunk). UDP rate-limiting is technically possible but rare; codec and DTMF are already-agreed concerns; concurrent calls don't apply because the call is up.

questions.prompt: The Ethernet Capture Tool in PSE produces what kind of file when you download a capture?
choices: A .pcap file that opens directly in Wireshark
A .tar archive containing one .pcap file inside
A .csv file with one row per SIP message
A .json file with structured call event data
questions.explanation: Output is a .tar archive containing one .pcap. Extract first, then open the .pcap in Wireshark. The other formats are wrong; CSV and JSON of SIP data come from other PSE reports, not the packet capture tool.

questions.prompt: CDR shows a failed outbound call with Last Participant = 'No matching route'. The user dialled 0299998888. What's the cause?
choices: The trunk to the carrier is in Registration Failed status
The user's extension isn't authorised to use any outbound route that matches the dialled pattern
The carrier returned 403 Forbidden
DTMF Mode mismatch dropped the digits the user dialled
questions.explanation: 'No matching route' means PSE evaluated outbound routes and didn't find one that both matched the dialled pattern AND included this user's extension in its From Extensions list. Often the route exists but the user's extension or group isn't in the allowed list.
